title: June 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19 | Stats NZ
content:
## June 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
03 September 2021, 10:45am
10 September 2021 : The last section of this paper, Summary of approaches for Gross domestic product: June 2021 quarter , was first published on 3 September 2021 and has now been updated.
June 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19 provides technical users with information about our approach to measuring the impact of COVID-19 in the forthcoming release of Gross domestic product: June 2021 quarter (scheduled for 16 September 2021).
This paper provides information about how we are responding to ongoing measurement challenges faced due to the nature of the economic shock caused by COVID-19. It includes an update on how we are managing the transition to a new design for the Quarterly Employment Survey (QES). This paper follows similar updates about our methodology during COVID-19 that have been published in advance of the release of Gross domestic product.
Information is presented in the following sections:
- Key events in the June 2021 quarter
- Data and methodology considerations
- Redesign of the Quarterly Employment Survey – update
- Summary of approaches for Gross domestic product: June 2021 quarter

## Key events in the June 2021 quarter
Globally, the COVID-19 pandemic continued to affect trade and travel flows. New Zealand’s border remained closed to most travellers throughout the quarter, with the exception of New Zealand citizens, residents with valid travel conditions, and those granted approval to travel. Quarantine-free travel between Australia and New Zealand was in place from 19 April 2021 to 23 July 2021. During this time there were some restrictions on travel to areas affected by COVID-19 cases.
Alert level changes in the quarter:
- Wellington moved to alert level 2 on 24 June 2021, following a traveller testing positive to COVID-19. No community cases were detected and Wellington moved to alert level 1 on 30 June 2021.

## Data and methodology considerations
The abrupt economic shock caused by COVID-19 and measures to contain it posed challenges to our normal methods of measuring economic activity. While the June 2021 quarter will be less impacted than some of the previous quarters, the measurement of some GDP(P) and GDP(E) components are still affected, and we continue to use alternative data and methodologies where this is the case. Where conditions support it, we are progressively returning to standard data sources and methods.
We released information on measurement challenges and our approach to these in the following papers:
COVID-19 lockdown and gross domestic product for March and June 2020 quarters
June 2020 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
June 2020 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19: Updated
September 2020 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
December 2020 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
March 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
We used alternative data sources in place of some of our usual data sources and methodologies in the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters and the March 2021 quarter to more accurately capture quarterly changes in activity. This was applied in the following cases:
- for components with methods that were unable to capture the sharp changes in activity that occurred due to the alert level 3 and 4 lockdowns (for example employment-based indicators, which were affected by the wage subsidy scheme)
- for components that experienced sharp changes in relationships between output and intermediate consumption
- for components that have a method of interpolation without a quarterly indicator.
During the September and December 2020 quarters and the March 2021 quarter we reviewed and reverted some components back to their usual quarterly method.
Due to the alert level 4 conditions in place during the September 2021 quarter, we have decided not to revert any components that are still using alternative indicators in the June 2021 quarter.
We will review all components in the September 2021 quarter and, where necessary, will again use alternative indicators to ensure the effects of the higher levels of restriction are appropriately measured.
Note that due to a changed approach to using the business financial data (BFD) in our alternative indicators (see Revisions to GDP(P) and GDP(E) for COVID-19-affected quarters ) we will be reviewing the treatment of all components in prior quarters (including past decisions to revert to the usual indicator). This may result in revisions.
The use of alternative indicators is summarised in Summary of approaches for Gross domestic product: June 2021 quarter . We will provide a further update on the approaches being used for GDP(P) and GDP(E) components on 10 September 2021, ahead of the release of Gross domestic product: June 2021 quarter.

### Revisions to GDP(P) and GDP(E) for COVID-19-affected quarters
As more data becomes available, we continue to review our approach to measuring GDP during the COVID-19-affected quarters. As part of this ongoing review process, we have recently identified some areas where we can improve our measurement over the COVID-19-affected period.
These changes will result in upward revisions to the level of GDP(P) in the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and the March 2021 quarter. These revisions are in the range of $100‒$600 million per quarter.
The June, September, and December 2020 quarters and the March 2021 quarter will see upward revisions to quarterly levels in the range $400‒$600 million.
The most significant revisions to quarterly growth will be to the June 2020 quarter. There will be minimal revisions to quarterly growth rates for the September and December 2020 quarters, and the March 2021 quarter.
The revisions will affect the average annual growth rate and same quarter previous year growth rates from the June 2020 quarter onwards.
There will be a smaller upward revision to GDP(E) of approximately $100‒150 million. This revision affects the March 2020 quarter level and growth rates.
Note that these approximate impacts have been calculated based on published data and are indicative only. They do not include other revisions that will be introduced as part of compiling Gross domestic product: June 2021 quarter – for example, we will be incorporating annual revisions from the Balance of Payments in this release, as we do every June quarter.
We will also be incorporating the latest balanced annual National accounts data in our September 2021 quarter release of Gross domestic product, due out in December 2021. This process occurs every September quarter and results in revisions to previously published series.
More detail on the sources of these revisions are below

#### Changes to transport support services methodology
The transport support services subindustry largely supplies services that are consumed by the other transport subindustries (such as road, rail, and air transport); ie its output is effectively the intermediate consumption of other sub-industries.
The existing method for this subindustry uses a weighted sum of other transport subindustries to derive a quarterly indicator series. The suitability of the quarterly indicator is dependent upon the assumption that the ratio of intermediate consumption to output in those subindustries does not change significantly on a quarterly basis. We recently identified that this assumption is not valid in COVID-19-affected periods, due mainly to the air transport subindustry seeing large shifts in its output to intermediate consumption ratio.
As a result we will temporarily change the quarterly method to using deflated sales data from the business financial data (BFD). This will result in upward revisions to levels for all quarters from March 2020 onwards.

#### Revisions to components using alternative data sources due to COVID-19
The alternative indicators that we have used to measure the effects of COVID-19 have been primarily sourced from the BFD. The availability of more data points since the initial COVID-19 shock has enabled us to assess activity over the last year, and review and refine our use of this data.
Where alternative indicators have been used during COVID-19-affected quarters, the standard approach has been to rate forward our existing series by the quarterly movements of the alternative indicator (usually BFD sales or BFD value added). This approach assumes that the BFD series and our usual indicator series are at similar levels (relative to their respective trends) in the base period, which in almost all cases has been the March 2020 quarter. An investigation found that there are some cases where BFD values were unusually high when compared with past trends in the series and with the usual indicator values in the March 2020 quarter.
Several components of GDP(P) and, to a lesser extent GDP(E), are affected by this change.
We will now use a base period of the December 2019 quarter to apply our alternative indicators, as in all cases our alternative and usual indicators align well with past trends in this quarter, making it an appropriate starting point.
This means that most components that adopted alternative indicators from the June 2020 quarter will now change to using alternative indicators in the March 2020 quarter as well.
In applying the alternative indicators from the December 2019 quarter onwards, we identified some challenges in using the March 2020 quarter BFD for two construction-related components; the construction services subindustry on the GDP(P) side, and the parts of the other construction component of gross fixed capital formation (GFKF) on the GDP(E) side that do not have quarterly input data.
In both cases the BFD data showed higher levels of activity in the March 2020 quarter than we consider is representative, when compared with the trends of their respective BFD series and other data we have confronted against. As a result we will adjust the BFD-sourced alternative indicator for this quarter as follows.
- For construction services we will use our usual indicator, which is the combined movement of non-residential and residential building construction sourced from value of building work put into place data.
- For the local government component of other construction GFKF we will reduce the activity from our usual method by the number of working days lost in level 4 lockdown.
- For the other smaller residual components of other construction GFKF that are not covered by input data, we will use our usual method.
The combined effect of the above, plus using the December 2019 quarter as our base period for applying the alternative indicators, will result in upward revisions to affected components, GDP(P), and GDP(E) (small impact to the latter). These revisions will affect levels from March 2020 quarter onwards, and mainly affect quarterly growth rates in the June 2020 quarter.

### Revisions to international visitor expenditure
As outlined in Changes to methodology for estimating visitor expenditure in New Zealand for the June 2021 quarter there have been revisions to estimates of international visitor expenditure due to a change in the methodology used to capture this spending during COVID-19-affected quarters. The new methodology produces more reliable estimates of spending by visitors in New Zealand when compared with the previous model and follows a recent review of the previous model.
These revisions apply from the June 2020 quarter onwards.
International visitor expenditure is an input into GDP(E). It contributes to the following components:
- exports of goods and services
- non-resident household expenditure in New Zealand
- household consumption expenditure (here we deduct non-resident household expenditure in New Zealand from domestic expenditure to get net domestic household expenditure)
The change will also impact lower-level published breakdowns (Classification of Individual Consumption according to Purpose, or COICOP basis) of household consumption expenditure estimates, boosting household consumption expenditure spending at this level in some COICOP divisions more than others.
Note that international visitor expenditure is offset within components of GDP(E), and as a result any revisions to international visitor expenditure will have minimal impact on total GDP(E).
Household consumption expenditure is a significant component of GDP(E). It also is an input into the derivation of household savings, for the following releases:
- official measures in the annual release of the institutional sector accounts
- quarterly experimental measures in the new release of national accounts (income, saving, assets, and liabilities).
All things being equal, changes to the methodology for estimating international visitor spending will have the following impacts.
- International visitor expenditure will see downward revisions from the June 2020 quarter onwards, with the March 2021 quarter most affected.
- They will cause downward revisions to exports of services, exports of goods and services, and non-resident household expenditure in New Zealand.
- Household consumption expenditure will revise up, and as a result household saving will revise down.
- The quarterly revisions will have a cumulative impact on the annual household saving estimates for the March 2021 year. Experimental estimates of this are published in National accounts (income, saving, assets, and liabilities): March 2021 quarter) . The first official estimate of household saving will be published in National accounts (income and expenditure), due to be released in November 2021.

### Challenges with measuring low-level breakdowns of household consumption expenditure
Our methodology for compiling low-level breakdowns of quarterly household consumption expenditure (HCE) is to measure total domestic spending for that category, then add New Zealand tourist expenditure overseas (travel debits) and remove overseas tourist expenditure in New Zealand (travel credits) to get HCE.
We use fixed proportions to split travel credits and debits into HCE categories. These proportions change based on commodity information from the annual supply-use balancing process but are generally quite stable over time and mostly based on historical information. As a result, these proportions are an area of weakness in our measurement. Since the June 2020 quarter we have made some use of electronic card transactions data to modify the proportions used to split travel credits into HCE categories. This allows us to account for some changes in spending patterns during the COVID-19-affected period. This approach was not possible for travel debits, due to a lack of data.
The abrupt changes in activity as a result of COVID-19 impacts have highlighted some limitations in our historical apportionment of travel credits to HCE classes. In recent quarters some HCE categories, notably hotels and accommodation, and recreation and cultural services, have seen quite large seasonally adjusted increases. These categories have previously had a relatively large portion of travel credits allocated to them in pre-COVID-19 times. This suggests that our pre-COVID-19 travel credit portions are likely to have been overestimated in these categories, and that consequently our HCE spending in these categories was previously underestimated. This mismeasurement affects the seasonal factors applied to these categories.
The large COVID-19-induced shifts in spending in categories such as hotels and accommodation, and recreation and cultural services (away from tourists, and toward spending by New Zealand residents) have hence translated into some strong seasonally adjusted movements for these HCE categories. While there have been strong increases, the extent of the increases is likely overstated in seasonally adjusted terms. We therefore advise caution when using data at this level of disaggregation.
Note that total HCE and total GDP(E) are not affected by these issues.
We will continue our use of additive outliers to treat unusual data points and will be testing the June 2021 quarter series to determine where additive outliers need to be applied. Previous outliers applied to March, June, September, December 2020 quarter and/or March 2021 quarter results will be retained in the upcoming release of Gross domestic product: June 2021 quarter.

### Seasonal adjustment of travel services and the June 2021 quarter
New Zealand’s border has been closed to almost all travellers since 19 March 2020, with the exception of New Zealand citizens, residents with valid travel conditions, and those granted approval to travel. Quarantine-free travel between Australia and New Zealand was in place from 19 April 2021 to 23 July 2021, and the impacts of this will be reflected in the upcoming release of Gross domestic product: June 2021 quarter , scheduled for release on 16 September 2021.
Exports of travel services make a significant contribution to New Zealand’s economy. Prior to COVID-19 the peak travel season fell in summer. In the 2019 calendar year, March quarter exports of travel services were worth $5.5 billion, compared with around $4.0 billion in the December quarter, $3.5 billion in the June quarter, and $3.0 billion in the September quarter.
The restrictions on travel to New Zealand in place since March 2020 mean that the value of travel service exports have declined significantly to around $1.0 billion in each of the June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and the March 2021 quarter.
The seasonal factors that we apply to GDP(E) account for the higher levels of exports of travel services usually observed in December and especially March quarters, and the lower levels usually observed in June and September quarters. Notably, in June quarters we normally observe a large fall in exports of travel following the March quarter peak.
Travel has historically been a strongly seasonal component, and so the seasonal factors in June quarters will rate activity up; conversely, in December and March quarters they rate activity down. Patterns of travel have changed dramatically since the pandemic – notably the closure of New Zealand’s border to most travellers since February 2020, and the introduction of quarantine-free travel with Australia in the June 2021 quarter.
In a multiplicative decomposition, the seasonal effects change proportionately with the trend. If the trend rises, so do the seasonal effects, while if the trend moves downward the seasonal effects diminish too. In an additive decomposition the seasonal effects remain broadly constant, no matter which direction the trend is moving in.
This means is that when the size of a component falls to very low levels – as has occurred to exports of travel services – the seasonal component delivered by the algorithm shrinks proportionally.
However, at the aggregate GDP(E) level we observe a smaller magnitude of change to the level of activity, as GDP(E) encompasses a range of other components. Therefore, the seasonal component at this level of aggregation is different. For example, in the March 2021 quarter while the level of travel services exports was low, total GDP(E) had returned to a level of activity similar to pre-COVID-19. As a result, the derived seasonal component in aggregate GDP(E) remains similar in size to pre-COVID quarters.
This means that seasonally aggregate GDP(E) will include the contribution of travel service exports in the June 2021 quarter, plus an additional upwards impact from the absence of usual travel activity, which under normal circumstances would see a fall in June quarters.
In GDP(P), travel-related activity is distributed across a range of industries, and appears to have a softer seasonal contribution than what we observe in GDP(E).
We have maintained the use of multiplicative seasonal adjustment in the treatment of GDP(P), GDP(E), and their component series, as it is the most suitable approach for the underlying characteristics of these series

## Redesign of the Quarterly Employment Survey – update
Data from the Quarterly Employment Survey (QES) is used as the quarterly indicator in a range of GDP(P) and GDP(E) components. Most QES-based components use total hours paid, with some components using QES employee counts as well. Depending on the component, the level or the movement of the QES is used.
In Gross domestic product: March 2021 quarter we used alternative data sources in place of the QES. The QES series used in compiling GDP(P) and GDP(E) are at a relatively detailed industry level, and the redesign caused some volatility at this level.
We have been able to resume our use of the QES as a quarterly indicator in the June 2021 quarter GDP for most components. This is because the March 2021 and June 2021 quarter measures are both on the new survey design and so quarterly movements are now on the same basis. Note that components using alternative data sources such as the BFD due to COVID-19-related impacts will continue to use these alternative indicators. These are outlined below in Summary of approaches for Gross domestic product: June 2021 quarter .

## Summary of approaches for Gross domestic product: June 2021 quarter
Please note that the information below is valid as at date of release and is subject to change. Note this section was first released on 3 September 2021 and was updated on 10 September 2021.
Nearly all components that have used or are still using alternative indicators sourced from the BFD will see revisions in the June 2021 quarter release of Gross domestic product. The BFD indicators are now being introduced from March 2020 quarter onwards (previously they were applied from June 2020 quarter onwards in most cases).
- Metal ore and non-metallic mineral mining and quarrying normally uses interpolation without a quarterly indicator. Derived value-added data from the BFD will be used for the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and in the March and June 2021 quarters.
Output indicators are normally sourced from value of building work put in place and are directly collected for parts of private-sector heavy and civil construction.
Changes for the components of this industry:
- QES is normally used for the public-sector component of heavy and civil engineering construction, while the private-sector component uses the movement in the other construction category of gross fixed capital formation (GFKF). Derived value-added volumes from the BFD were used in the June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and in the March and June 2021 quarters.
- Construction services is normally derived from the combined movement in residential and non-residential building construction, which is sourced from value of building work put in place data. BFD data indicates issues in gross output and intermediate consumption ratios, and so derived BFD value-added data was used in the June and September quarters, and in the March 2021 quarter. We initially used the BFD for December 2020 quarter and March 2021 quarter, but the change in method of applying this data – see Revisions to GDP(P) and GDP(E) in COVID-19-affected quarters section above – has indicated that the December 2020 quarter and March 2021 quarter should be reverted back to their usual indicator. This will be implemented in the June 2021 quarter release of gross domestic product.

#### Electricity, gas, water and waste services
Changes for the components of this industry:
- Waste collection, treatment, and disposal normally uses QES. We used derived BFD sales volumes for the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and in the March and June 2021 quarters.

#### Retail trade, accommodation, and food services
All subindustries in this industry normally use output indicators sourced from retail trade survey data. The following subindustries used derived BFD value-added data (including change in stock levels) to account for changes in gross output and intermediate consumption in the June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and in the March and June 2021 quarters:
- recreational, clothing, footwear, and personal accessory retail services
- food and beverage services
- accommodation services.

#### Transport, postal, and warehousing services
Changes for the components of this industry:
- Air and space transport normally use passenger and freight revenue kilometres data, collected directly from major companies. We will use derived BFD value-added volumes in the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and March and June 2021 quarters to account for changes in the relationship between gross output and intermediate consumption.
- Scenic and sightseeing transport normally uses international visitor arrivals data. We will use BFD sales-volumes data in the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and March and June 2021 quarters.
- Other transport normally uses interpolation without a quarterly indicator. Derived BFD value-added volumes will be used to account for changes in the relationship between gross output and intermediate consumption in the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and the March and June 2021 quarters.
- Warehousing and storage services normally uses interpolation without a quarterly indicator. We will use derived BFD valued-added volumes for the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and the March and June 2021 quarters.
- Transport support services normally uses a composite indicator based on other transport subindustries. We will use derived BFD sales volumes for the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and the March and June 2021 quarters. There will be revisions to this component, due to changes in our method for COVID-19 affected quarters – see Revisions to GDP(P) and GDP(E) in COVID-19-affected quarters section above.

#### Information, media, and telecommunications
Changes for the components of this industry:
- Publishing (except internet and music publishing) normally uses QES. Derived BFD value-added volumes will be used in the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and the March and June 2021 quarters.
- Broadcasting and internet publishing normally uses gross earnings from the employer monthly schedule (EMS), which will overstate work done due to the impact of the wage subsidy. Derived BFD value-added values will be used for the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and the March and June 2021 quarters.

#### Professional, scientific, technical, administrative, and support services
Changes for the components of this industry:
- Professional, scientific, and technical services normally uses GST sales data. Derived BFD value-added volumes will be used in the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and the March and June 2021 quarters.
- Advertising, market research, and management services normally uses derived BFD sales-volumes. We used derived BFD value-added in the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters and in the March and June 2021 quarters.
- Travel agency and tour arrangement services normally uses QES indicators. In the March 2020 quarter, GST sales data was used to represent the change in activity. Deflated value-added data derived from the BFD was used for the June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and the March and June 2021 quarters.
- Employment and other administrative services normally use QES. We will use derived BFD value-added volumes to account for changes in gross output and intermediate consumption ratios in the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and the March and June 2021 quarters.
- Building, cleaning, pest control, and other support services normally use QES indicators. We will use derived BFD sales volumes for the March and June 2020 quarters.

#### Education and training
All education and training subindustries normally use a method of interpolation of the annual without a quarterly indicator. The following approaches will be used to represent activity in this industry:
- Preschool education was estimated in the March 2020 quarter using an assumption of no activity for the days of the alert level 4 lockdown, and 100 percent activity at alert level 1. For the June and September 2020 quarters we used cumulative attendance hours. For the December 2020 quarter, we reverted to the usual method of interpolation of the annual with no indicator. In the March and June 2021 quarters we will look at cumulative attendance hours again due to the alert level changes that occurred.
- Tertiary education was estimated using student visa data in the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters and the March 2021 quarter, and will be again in the June 2021 quarter. We assume that remote learning undertaken during higher alert levels is equivalent to face-to-face learning.
- Adult, community, and other education was adjusted using unit-level information to estimate activity during alert level 4 in the March 2020 quarter. Derived BFD sales volumes will be used in the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters and the March and June 2021 quarters.

#### Healthcare and social assistance
The following subindustries normally use QES indicators. See below changes to methods made in previous quarters.
- The market component of hospital services used derived BFD sales volumes in the June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and the March 2021 quarter.
- The market component of private medical and other healthcare services used a composite indicator derived from low-level household labour force survey data and monthly employment indicator data in the June 2020 quarter.
- Residential care services and social assistance will use derived BFD sales volumes in the June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and the March and June 2021 quarters.

#### Arts, recreation, and other services
The following components of this industry used alternative indicators in previous quarters.
- Heritage and artistic activities normally uses QES. We will use derived BFD sales volumes in the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters. We will use EMS data in the March and June 2021 quarters.
- Sport and recreation activities normally uses QES. We will use derived BFD sales volumes in the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and the March and June 2021 quarters.
- Civil, professional, and other interest groups normally uses QES. We will use derived BFD value added in the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters.
In cases where changes have been made to line up to corresponding components on the production side of GDP, the different underlying concepts between measurement of production and expenditure have been accounted for.

#### Household consumption expenditure
- For HCE on education, domestic expenditure was aligned with the approaches used for GDP(P) education subindustries in the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and the March and June 2021 quarters. The measurement of education travel credits is consistent with the approach used in our balance of payments.
- Hospital services and outpatient services were adjusted to align with health and social assistance, using derived BFD sales values.
- Recreational services and cultural services both normally use QES indicators, in line with heritage and artistic activities and sport and recreation activities in the arts and recreation industry. These subindustries used derived BFD sales values in the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and the March and June 2021 quarters.
- Road passenger services and other passenger transport costs were both adjusted using electronic card transactions (ECT) data in March 2020 quarter. Low-level BFD sales-values data will be used in the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and the March and June 2021 quarters.
- We updated travel credit ratios to several categories in the June 2020 quarter and have maintained these ratios in the September and December 2020 quarters, and the March and June 2021 quarters.

#### Government final consumption expenditure
As noted in March 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19 we identified that managed isolation quarantine and emergency housing expenditure has previously been captured in household consumption expenditure instead of government final consumption expenditure, and we incorporated revisions in Gross domestic product: March 2021 quarter to reflect the correct treatment. We have ensured that both these items have been appropriately classified in our June 2021 quarter results.

#### Gross fixed capital formation
The local government component of other construction uses interpolation without a quarterly indicator. In the March 2020 quarter, an adjustment was made to account for the days in alert level 4 lockdown. In the June, September, and December 2020 quarters, sales data from the BFD was used.
Parts of the private and central government components of other construction use interpolation without a quarterly indicator. In the June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and March and June 2021 quarters, sales data from the BFD will be used.
Land improvements usually use interpolation without a quarterly indicator. We used low-level movements from other construction to represent this component in the June, September, and December 2020 quarters and the March and June 2021 quarters.
Research and development normally uses interpolation with the EMS as a quarterly indicator. In the March and June 2020 quarters, sales data from the BFD has been used.
Tourist spending in New Zealand is normally estimated using data collected via the International Visitor Survey (IVS).
IVS data collection has ceased because of border closures introduced from March 2020. A model has been used to estimate this expenditure since the June 2020 quarter. There will be revisions to these estimates due to a change in the model being used to estimate this expenditure. See Change to methodology for estimating visitor expenditure in New Zealand for the June 2021 quarter for further information about these revisions.
For education export services, we estimate the tuition fees paid by overseas students from export education levy (EEL) data compiled by the Ministry of Education (MoE).
For the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and the March and June 2021 quarters we used updated 2019 EEL data from MoE and adjusted it using updated student visa numbers from Immigration New Zealand. See International trade: June 2020 quarter data sources and methods.
Ruvani Ratnayake 027 445 3174 info@stats.govt.nz
